title: The House: Four Rooms You Cannot Skip
paragraphs: Think of approaching someone in conflict the way you’d approach their house. You wouldn’t kick the door in. You wouldn’t barge past the hallway and park yourself in their lounge room uninvited. There’s a sequence. There are rooms. Each room earns the next. Skip one, and you’ll get shown the door.
Room One: The Knock

This is everything that happens before the real conversation begins. The text you send. The tone of your voice when you say "Can we talk?" The timing you choose. The look on your face when they glance up from the other side of the glass.

Most conflict conversations fail right here. At the door. Before a single meaningful word has been spoken. They fail because people either pound on the door — aggressive, ambush-style, loaded with emotion — or they don’t knock at all. One is a battering ram. The other is cowardice dressed up as patience. Both leave the elephant exactly where it was.
A good knock is gentle, clear, and gives the other person time to prepare.
Proverbs 15:1 — "A gentle answer turns away wrath, but a harsh word stirs up anger."

That verse isn’t just ancient wisdom. It’s neuroscience before neuroscience had a name. A gentle tone lowers the cortisol response in the listener. A harsh tone triggers fight-or-flight before you’ve said a single sentence of substance. The knock sets the temperature for everything that follows. Blow the knock, and the door stays shut. Maybe for months.
The Biblical Principle: Go First. Go Humble.

Matthew 5:23–24 says something radical. If you’re on your way to worship and you remember that someone has something against you, Jesus says leave your offering at the altar and go be reconciled first. Let that land. God says your worship can wait. The relationship cannot. The initiative belongs to the one who’s aware there’s a fracture — not necessarily the one who caused it. The one who sees it.
Room Two: The Foyer

They’ve opened the door. You’re in the entryway. This is not where you drop the bomb. This is where you read the room. The foyer is small talk that isn’t small. It’s the sixty seconds where you communicate three things without saying them: I’m not here to attack you. I’m not here to win. I’m here because this relationship matters to me.
James 1:19 — "Everyone should be quick to listen, slow to speak, and slow to become angry."

Quick to listen. That’s the foyer in three words. You’re not talking yet. You’re watching. Arms crossed? Eyes darting? Monosyllabic answers? That’s a person who hasn’t decided it’s safe yet. Don’t push. Stay warm. Ask how they’re doing. Mean it. The foyer can take two minutes or twenty. Let them set the pace.
Room Three: The Kitchen

The kitchen is where it gets real. You’ve earned enough trust to move past pleasantries. This is the room where you share something vulnerable about yourself first. Not the accusation. Not the complaint. Your part. Your feelings. Your failures.
Matthew 7:3–5 — "Why do you look at the speck of sawdust in your brother’s eye and pay no attention to the plank in your own eye?"

That verse is not just about humility. It’s about sequence. Deal with your plank first. Not because their speck doesn’t matter. But because owning your plank earns you the moral authority to speak into theirs. Here’s the paradox that will shock you every time: the more honestly you own your part, the more likely the other person is to own theirs.
What to Watch For

The kitchen is where conversations get hijacked. This is the testing ground. They’re watching you. Can I trust this person? Is this a trap? If they deflect, gently bring it back. If they escalate, stay calm. If they shut down, give them space but don’t walk out. Whatever you do, don’t react to their defence mechanism. Respond to the person underneath it.
Room Four: The Lounge Room
If you’ve earned the foyer and survived the kitchen, you arrive here. Sit down. Get comfortable. And say the thing.
Not a complaint. Not a verdict. A conversation.
"Here’s what I’ve been experiencing. Here’s how it’s affected me. Here’s what I think we both need. And here’s what I’m willing to change."
Ephesians 4:15 — "Instead, speaking the truth in love, we will grow to become in every respect the mature body of him who is the head, that is, Christ."

Truth without love is brutality. Love without truth is sentimentality. The lounge room demands both. In the same breath. Don’t pile on. One issue. The moment you introduce a second grievance, you’ve moved from conversation to prosecution and you’ve lost the room. And don’t rush to resolution. Sometimes the lounge room is about being heard. The fix comes later.
